agent_output: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"HTTP ingestion endpoint","to":"internal event pipeline","status":"correct","issue":"Structured logging with request IDs enables end-to-end tracing through ingestion path"},{"from":"event pipeline","to":"data warehouse","status":"broken","issue":"Non-idempotent writes combined with at-least-once delivery guarantees duplicate records in the warehouse under normal operation. Every retry, every lost ACK, every circuit breaker recovery produces duplicates. This is not an edge case — it is a steady-state data integrity violation."},{"from":"event pipeline","to":"dead-letter queue","status":"broken","issue":"DLQ has no consumer. Failed events silently accumulate with no processing, no alerting, and no recovery path. This is equivalent to silent data loss with extra steps."},{"from":"retry mechanism","to":"data warehouse","status":"broken","issue":"Exponential backoff retries re-send events that may have already been written. Without idempotency keys or deduplication, each retry attempt can create a new duplicate record."}],"contract_compliance":"The service promises event delivery but cannot guarantee exactly-once semantics. Downstream consumers and analytics will operate on inflated data. Any metric derived from this pipeline (click counts, conversion rates, funnel analysis) is unreliable.","data_flow_integrity":"Fundamentally compromised. The combination of at-least-once delivery, non-idempotent writes, and no deduplication means the data warehouse will accumulate duplicates proportional to retry frequency and network instability. Under load or partial failures — exactly when accuracy matters most — duplication rate increases."},"observability_review":{"logging":{"coverage":"adequate","gaps":[]},"metrics":{"coverage":"Strong — Prometheus latency histograms, p99 dashboards, and alerts configured","gaps":["No metric tracking duplicate event rate or deduplication failures","No metric on DLQ depth or DLQ age of oldest message","No metric distinguishing genuinely new writes from retried writes"]},"tracing":{"coverage":"Good — OpenTelemetry distributed tracing in place","gaps":["Traces should tag retried spans to distinguish first-attempt writes from retry writes"]},"debugging_capability":"Good for latency and availability issues. Poor for data integrity issues — an on-call engineer at 3am has no way to detect or quantify duplication, and no way to know the DLQ is silently filling up."},"resilience_assessment":{"failure_modes":[{"scenario":"Data warehouse temporarily unavailable","handling":"partial","recommendation":"Circuit breaker and retries handle availability, but retries produce duplicates on recovery. Add an idempotency key (e.g., event_id + timestamp hash) to warehouse writes so retries are safe."},{"scenario":"ACK lost after successful write","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Event is re-sent and written again. Implement server-side deduplication using a unique event identifier with a TTL-based dedup cache or upsert/merge semantics in the warehouse."},{"scenario":"DLQ fills up","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"No consumer, no alert, no backpressure. Events rot indefinitely. Deploy a DLQ consumer with alerting on queue depth > threshold and age of oldest message > SLA."},{"scenario":"Sustained high retry rate during degraded warehouse performance","handling":"partial","recommendation":"Retries work but each creates duplicates. Under sustained degradation this compounds — a 5-minute warehouse brownout at 10k events/sec with 3 retries could produce up to 90 million duplicate records. Idempotency is mandatory."},{"scenario":"DLQ storage exhaustion","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"With no consumer and no TTL, the DLQ will eventually exhaust storage. Add a retention policy, a consumer, and an alert on queue size."}],"retry_config":"Exponential backoff with 3 retries — mechanically sound but unsafe without idempotent writes","timeout_config":"Not specified but assumed present given load test results","circuit_breakers":"Present on warehouse writes — good","graceful_degradation":"Partial — circuit breaker prevents cascade but DLQ is a black hole with no recovery path"},"operational_readiness":{"deployment_ready":false,"rollback_capable":true,"rollback_plan":"Blue/green deployment with load balancer traffic switch enables instant rollback. This is well-designed.","monitoring_coverage":"Strong for availability and latency. Blind spot on data integrity: no duplicate detection, no DLQ depth monitoring.","on_call_documentation":"Needs runbook entries for: (1) how to detect and remediate duplicate records, (2) how to drain and reprocess the DLQ, (3) what the expected duplicate rate is under normal vs degraded operation","new_infrastructure_required":{"new_env_vars":[],"new_services":["DLQ consumer/processor service"],"new_queues_topics":[],"config_changes":["Add DLQ depth and age alerting rules","Add idempotency key configuration for warehouse writes"],"infra_provisioning":["Deduplication store (e.g., Redis with TTL) or warehouse upsert/merge capability"]},"database_safety":{"has_migrations":false,"reversible":false,"data_preserving":false,"large_table_impact":true,"details":"Non-idempotent writes to a data warehouse will cause unbounded row growth from duplicates. Cleaning up duplicates in a large analytical warehouse is expensive and operationally risky. The longer this runs without deduplication, the harder remediation becomes."},"verdict":"NOT READY"},"escalations":[{"type":"integration_gap","issue":"Non-idempotent data warehouse writes with at-least-once delivery guarantee duplicate records under normal operation","reason":"This is a fundamental data integrity flaw, not an edge case. The retry mechanism, circuit breaker recovery, and at-least-once semantics all produce duplicates by design. Every downstream analytic will be wrong. Fix requires either: (1) idempotency keys with upsert/merge writes, (2) a deduplication layer before the warehouse, or (3) both. This must be resolved before production traffic touches this pipeline.","routed_to":"MJ"},{"type":"integration_gap","issue":"Dead-letter queue has no consumer, no alerting, and no recovery path","reason":"The DLQ exists but is a write-only black hole. Failed events are never reprocessed, never alerted on, and will silently accumulate until storage is exhausted. An on-call engineer has no visibility into this. This needs a consumer service, a reprocessing strategy, and alerts on queue depth and message age.","routed_to":"MJ"}],"confidence":{"level":60,"high_confidence_areas":["Observability stack is well-implemented","Deployment and rollback strategy is production-grade","Load testing validates throughput capacity","The duplicate data problem is real and will manifest immediately in production"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact duplicate rate under normal operation — depends on network conditions and ACK loss frequency","Whether the data warehouse supports native upsert/merge that could simplify the fix","DLQ storage limits and retention policy"],"assumptions":["The data warehouse does not have built-in deduplication on ingest","Event IDs or natural keys exist that could serve as idempotency keys","Downstream analytics consumers have no independent deduplication"]}}

content.thinking:
Let me assess this real-time analytics ingestion service based on the signals provided.

Classification: `none` — sufficient context exists to make a determination.

Key issues:
1. Non-idempotent writes + at-least-once delivery = guaranteed data corruption over time
2. Dead-letter queue with no consumer = silent data loss
3. These are fundamental data integrity issues, not nice-to-haves
